title: The Feeling Acknowledged, the Feeling at Rest
subtitle: A visualisation for releasing emotional residue before sleep
excerpt: A five-minute visualisation for Day 6 of the sleep arc. The scene gives each feeling from the day a moment to be seen, then places it somewhere outside the body so it doesn't need to travel through the night.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Built from the Day 6 theme of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798269649795): easing emotional overload at bedtime.

practice.whenToUse: When you have feelings left from the day that haven't had a chance to move through — use this lying down in bed, after the day is done.

content.text: A short visualisation for Day 6 of the sleep arc. Use it lying down in bed. Eyes closed. The image gives each feeling from the day a place to rest outside of you.
Picture a plain ceramic bowl on a table beside the bed. Nothing elaborate. It's there to hold what you don't need to carry through the night.

Think back through the day. Find one feeling that hasn't finished moving. It might be from a conversation, a moment that went oddly, something you didn't say. Name it plainly — frustration, disappointment, low-level anxiety, leftover irritation.
Picture placing it in the bowl. Not suppressing it — acknowledging it and giving it somewhere to sit that isn't inside you. You can come back to it. It isn't going anywhere.
Do this for each feeling that comes up. Name it. Place it in the bowl. Notice the weight of it leaving.
When the bowl is full, picture a cloth going over it. The feelings are there; they aren't going to startle you or need your attention tonight. Sleep is its own time.
Original to homemade.education. Built from the Day 6 theme of
(Rebecca J Page, 2025). The containment image (bowl, cloth) draws on a widely used technique in body-based therapeutic approaches for emotion regulation.
